name: Texas Dept of Transportation (TxDOT)
tagline: Texas's statewide transportation agency and one of the largest in the nation — a sprawling field, edge, sensor, ITS, and data footprint built around a 10-year, $100B+ Unified Transportation Program and an AI plan now moving from pilots into daily operations.
overview: The Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT) plans, builds, operates, and maintains the state highway system — one of the largest transportation networks in the country, spanning roughly 80,000 centerline miles across 25 districts. Headquartered in Austin, it is a heavy edge and field organization: traffic management centers, roadside ITS and sensor infrastructure, signals, cameras, connected-vehicle pilots, and a large back-office data and applications estate. Its capital program runs through the annually updated Unified Transportation Program (UTP), a 10-year 'road map' that the draft 2026 edition put at roughly $101.6B — the third straight year above $100B. On the technology side, TxDOT's Information Technology Division supports more than 700 IT professionals and the agency has moved aggressively on AI: its AI Strategic Plan, launched December 2024 and updated in February 2026, frames AI as a 'force multiplier' embedded in workflows under a 'human-led, AI-supported' governance model. For a Dell SLG seller, TxDOT is an infrastructure-, edge-, and data-rich account whose buying runs through TxDOT's own procurement plus DIR Cooperative Contracts, gated by TX-RAMP and TxDOT's internal IS-RAMP requirements.

budget.intro: TxDOT's scale shows up in its capital program, not a small IT line. The draft 2026 UTP was about $101.6B over 10 years (roughly $7.5B of it new funding), and Governor Abbott has separately framed a broader transportation investment of over $146B. Embedded in the capital plan is a meaningful traffic-technology / ITS component — the kind of sensor, edge, and data infrastructure a compute and storage vendor maps to.

stats.label: Draft 2026 UTP (10-yr)
stats.note: Third consecutive year above $100B; ~$7.5B of new funding

stats.label: Total transportation investment
stats.note: Broader plan figure cited by Gov. Abbott

stats.label: Traffic-tech in draft 2026 UTP
stats.note: New traffic-management technology / ITS projects (per GovTech, Jul 2025)

notes: The UTP is updated annually and approved by the Texas Transportation Commission — figures shift between draft and adopted versions, so confirm against the final adopted UTP before forecasting.
TxDOT is a large direct technology spender in its own right (700+ IT pros, statewide ITS/edge), distinct from DIR, which is the procurement path rather than the end customer.

initiatives.name: AI Strategic Plan — pilots to operations
initiatives.summary: Launched Dec 2024 and updated Feb 2026, TxDOT is moving AI from isolated pilots into everyday operations under a 'human-led, AI-supported' model. It has evaluated 200+ use cases via a Readiness Scorecard, with live examples like AI incident detection in Austin. Strong fit for AI-infrastructure, data-platform, and edge-compute conversations.

initiatives.name: Connected & autonomous vehicles / ITS
initiatives.summary: TxDOT runs a Connected and Autonomous Vehicle Task Force and a growing intelligent-transportation-systems footprint — roadside sensors, traffic management centers, signal tech, and connected-vehicle pilots. The draft 2026 UTP earmarks $264M+ in new traffic-management technology. This is an edge, sensor-data, and real-time-processing buildout.

initiatives.name: Innovation & testing platforms
initiatives.summary: Programs such as the Texas Innovation Alliance (analytics and AI for smart transportation) and proposed neutral testing/proving-ground concepts position TxDOT as a public-private innovation buyer — a route for piloting compute, data, and AI solutions before scaling.

initiatives.name: Cybersecurity & data protection (IS-RAMP)
initiatives.summary: TxDOT operates its own Information Security Risk and Authorization Management Program (IS-RAMP) and a controls-standards catalog, with a CISO who reviews and approves technology proposals. Security and TX-RAMP posture is a gate to clear on any data-touching deal.

procurement.intro: TxDOT buys both through its own agency procurement and through statewide vehicles. Two early checks for any technology play: that Dell's DIR Cooperative Contracts are active and correctly scoped for TxDOT, and that any cloud / as-a-service offering meets TX-RAMP and TxDOT's internal IS-RAMP security requirements.

vehicles.name: TxDOT agency procurement
vehicles.detail: TxDOT runs its own solicitations and contracts for goods, services, and IT — distinct from DIR. Larger and program-specific buys typically flow through the agency's own procurement and the Texas Transportation Commission's approvals.

vehicles.name: DIR Cooperative Contracts
vehicles.detail: The statewide pre-competed vehicle TxDOT can buy IT hardware, software, and services through without running a separate RFP — a common path for technology purchases.

vehicles.name: TX-RAMP / IS-RAMP certification
vehicles.detail: TX-RAMP is the statewide cloud-security gate; TxDOT additionally enforces its own IS-RAMP requirements for third parties that access, store, or transmit TxDOT data. Clear the right level before pursuing cloud / aaS deals.

powerStructure.name: Marc D. Williams, P.E.
powerStructure.title: Executive Director
powerStructure.note: Agency leader (22nd Executive Director); has publicly positioned AI as 'a strategic asset embedded in our workflows.' The top relationship for the account.

powerStructure.name: Anh Selissen
powerStructure.title: Chief Information Officer & IT Division Director
powerStructure.note: Leads 700+ IT professionals across security, operations, applications, data governance, and traffic-technology operations; CIO since Sept 2019. Recognized CIO/State IT Leader of the Year and, in Nov 2025, appointed by Gov. Abbott to the state's Public Sector AI Systems Advisory Board (term to Sept 1, 2027) — the central technology relationship and a statewide-AI voice.

powerStructure.name: TxDOT IT Division — Cybersecurity / CISO
powerStructure.title: Security policy, IS-RAMP, approvals
powerStructure.note: TxDOT's CISO function reviews and approves technology proposals and owns the IS-RAMP / controls-standards requirements vendors must meet. (Individual CISO name not publicly confirmed; reports up through the IT Division.)

powerStructure.name: Texas Transportation Commission
powerStructure.title: Governing body (W. Alvin New, Acting Chair)
powerStructure.note: Five-member, Governor-appointed commission that adopts the UTP and approves major contracts and program funding. As of mid-2026 W. Alvin New is listed as Acting Chair (interim — verify current status); other commissioners: Robert C. Vaughn, Alejandro 'Alex' G. Meade III, Steven D. Alvis, Pat Gordon.

news.title: Commission advances draft 2027 UTP (~$95B / 10-yr, ~$5B new); public comment Jul 3-Aug 3, adoption Aug 2026
news.impact: The next-cycle capital envelope that supersedes the 2026 UTP as the current plan; ~$5B new funding plus continued Clear Lanes and connectivity initiatives frame downstream ITS, sensor, and field-technology spend. Track adoption in August.

news.title: TxDOT launches SH 130 Smart Corridor (4+ mi; 33 poles / 66 sensors; cameras, LTE, fiber, AI)
news.impact: One of the most advanced U.S. smart corridors: lane-level awareness feeding real-time TxDOT systems and connected/autonomous vehicles (Tesla, Waymo), with expansion planned toward Georgetown, Mustang Ridge and eventually I-35. Direct fit for edge compute, sensor-data ingest, and real-time processing.

news.title: TxDOT updates AI Strategic Plan; multi-cloud exploration, Copilot to 940+ staff, Rekor AI in Austin
news.impact: AI moving from pilots to embedded operations across 30+ initiatives; explicit move toward a multi-cloud, multi-platform environment and generative-AI rollout — durable demand for AI infrastructure, data platforms, and edge compute. (Plan update variously dated Jan/Feb 2026.)

news.title: CIO Anh Selissen appointed to Gov. Abbott's Public Sector AI Systems Advisory Board (term to Sept 2027)
news.impact: TxDOT's central technology relationship now also shapes statewide public-sector AI policy — reinforces AI as a durable, top-of-agenda buying theme and a strategic executive relationship.

news.title: Draft 2026 UTP includes $264M+ in new traffic-management technology
news.impact: Confirms a funded ITS / sensor / edge buildout inside the capital program — a mappable infrastructure and data opportunity.

dellActions: Confirm Dell's DIR Cooperative Contract coverage and reseller scope for TxDOT, and identify TxDOT's own agency procurement path for larger program-specific buys.
Lead with AI-infrastructure, data-platform, and edge-compute messaging tied directly to TxDOT's AI Strategic Plan (pilots-to-operations) and its 'human-led, AI-supported' governance model.
Map the ITS / connected-vehicle / traffic-technology buildout (the $264M+ in the draft 2026 UTP) to edge, ruggedized, sensor-data, and real-time-processing solutions.
Engage CIO Anh Selissen's IT Division early and clear TX-RAMP plus TxDOT's internal IS-RAMP security requirements before pursuing any cloud / as-a-service deal.
Track the adopted (final) 2026 UTP versus the draft, and the Texas Transportation Commission's approvals, to time forecasts and major program pursuits.
